From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

"The Other People" redirects here. For other uses, see [Other People](/wiki/Other_People_(disambiguation) "Other People (disambiguation)").

1968 British film

| Sleep Is Lovely | |
| --- | --- |
| [Olga Georges-Picot](/wiki/Olga_Georges-Picot "Olga Georges-Picot") and [Donald Pleasence](/wiki/Donald_Pleasence "Donald Pleasence") in a scene from the film[[1]](#cite_note-BFI-1) | |
| Directed by | [David Hart](/wiki/David_Hart_(UK_political_activist) "David Hart (UK political activist)") |
| Written by | David Hart Michael Josef |
| Produced by | [Stanley Baker](/wiki/Stanley_Baker "Stanley Baker") [Michael Deeley](/wiki/Michael_Deeley "Michael Deeley") |
| Starring | [Peter McEnery](/wiki/Peter_McEnery "Peter McEnery") [Donald Pleasence](/wiki/Donald_Pleasence "Donald Pleasence") |
| Cinematography | Brian Probyn |
| Edited by | Peter Pitt |
| Music by | [Johnny Dankworth](/wiki/Johnny_Dankworth "Johnny Dankworth") |
| Production companies | [Oakhurst Productions](/wiki/Oakhurst_Productions "Oakhurst Productions") [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") Telstar Productions |
| Release date | * 1968 (1968) |
| Running time | 89 minutes |
| Country | United Kingdom |
| Language | English |
| Budget | £80,000[[1]](#cite_note-BFI-1) |

***Sleep Is Lovely*** (also known as ***The Other People*** and ***I Love You, I Hate You***) is a 1968 British film directed by [David Hart](/wiki/David_Hart_(political_activist) "David Hart (political activist)") and starring [Peter McEnery](/wiki/Peter_McEnery "Peter McEnery"), [Donald Pleasence](/wiki/Donald_Pleasence "Donald Pleasence") and [Olga Georges-Picot](/wiki/Olga_Georges-Picot "Olga Georges-Picot").[[2]](#cite_note-BFIsearch-2) It was written by Hart and Michael Josef. The film appears to have never been released, and is considered a [lost film](/wiki/Lost_film "Lost film").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sleep_Is_Lovely&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

Peter can't get over his ex-girlfriend Elsa even though they broke up over a year ago. He spends all his time on a barge owned by his friend John and John's younger brother Colin. One morning Peter, John and Colin see a middle aged man, Clive, fall out of a motor cruiser into the water. They rescue him and decide to ransom him for £1,000. Peter and Elsa are-reunited but Elsa then commences an affair with Colin. Clive turns out to be Elsa's father.

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sleep_Is_Lovely&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast")]

* [Peter McEnery](/wiki/Peter_McEnery "Peter McEnery") as Peter
* [Donald Pleasence](/wiki/Donald_Pleasence "Donald Pleasence") as Clive, Elsa's father
* [Olga Georges-Picot](/wiki/Olga_Georges-Picot "Olga Georges-Picot") as Elsa
* [John McEnery](/wiki/John_McEnery "John McEnery") as John
* [George Coulouris](/wiki/George_Coulouris "George Coulouris") as police inspector
* [Bruce Robinson](/wiki/Bruce_Robinson "Bruce Robinson") as Colin
* [Colin Jeavons](/wiki/Colin_Jeavons "Colin Jeavons") as butler
* William Ellis as Royal Marines officer
* [Virginia Wetherell](/wiki/Virginia_Wetherell "Virginia Wetherell") as girl at airport

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sleep_Is_Lovely&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

Producer [Michael Deeley](/wiki/Michael_Deeley "Michael Deeley") said director [David Hart](/wiki/David_Hart_(UK_political_activist) "David Hart (UK political activist)") "was one of the cleverest men I have met and when he decided to be a film director it seemed like a good idea for me to help him."[[4]](#cite_note-Deeley-4) The film was set up at Deeley's [Oakhurst Productions](/wiki/Oakhurst_Productions "Oakhurst Productions") and financed by [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") as part of a low-budget film state ordered by the studio's new owner, [Charles Bluhdorn](/wiki/Charles_Bluhdorn "Charles Bluhdorn").[[1]](#cite_note-BFI-1)[[5]](#cite_note-bake-5)

The film was passed to the [BBFC](/wiki/British_Board_of_Film_Classification "British Board of Film Classification") for certification in September 1968,[[6]](#cite_note-6) but despite the cast involved and backing of [Oakhurst Productions](/wiki/Oakhurst_Productions "Oakhurst Productions") and [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), it does not appear to have had a trade screening, been shown to a paying audience, screened on TV or released on video.[[1]](#cite_note-BFI-1)
